frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars
generated_comment: Look, Section 498A was enacted with a very necessary purpose, and I have seen genuine cruelty cases where the woman had absolutely no protection except the law. But after some years on the family court bench, one also learns that many complaints are filed only after matrimonial negotiations fail, and then every member of the husband’s family is implicated, whether they were involved or not. In my experience, real abuse usually leaves a consistent pattern in conduct and evidence; quite a few educated women, especially when advised by family, tend to use these provisions as pressure tactics in what is essentially a marital dispute. So yes, misuse exists—serious misuse—and courts must be careful not to treat every allegation as gospel simply because it is made by a wife.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women complainants as strategically dishonest and sets a higher credibility burden on wives alleging abuse. It encodes the legal-system weaponisation narrative through skepticism of women's testimony and the assumption that many DV/dowry cases are bargaining tools rather than genuine harm.
persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 56. Retired. A former judge in Chennai. You have presided over hundreds of family court cases including domestic violence and dowry cases. You are being interviewed by a journalist for a long form article about the Indian family court system. The journalist asks you whether you think Section 498A is misused. Your answer Ñ shaped by decades of actually sitting in judgment over these cases, seeing the evidence, watching both sides, making decisions Ñ is more complicated than either the men's rights narrative or the feminist narrative. But in the course of your answer you reveal specific assumptions about women's credibility, about what constitutes real abuse, and about what the purpose of these laws should be, that show how even a person with genuine legal expertise can carry the cultural bias of a lifetime into a courtroom.
